nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated rocky river sections with clear, warm, soft acidic water; found clinging to boulders and rock faces in rapids and riffles

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rock stacks for hiding and territory
caves and crevices (ceramic cave tubes, coconut shells, or slate stacks)
driftwood as additional cover and biofilm surface
strong water flow (powerhead or wavemaker) to replicate Orinoco rapids

diet.notes: Carnivore-leaning omnivore — unlike algae-grazing plecos (e.g. Pterygoplichthys), the Blue Phantom Pleco feeds primarily on protein-rich foods. Accepts high-quality sinking carnivore pellets, frozen and freeze-dried bloodworms, brine shrimp, and mysis shrimp. Also grazes biofilm and aufwuchs from rocks; some vegetable matter (zucchini, cucumber, blanched spinach) is accepted but should not form the bulk of the diet. Driftwood in the tank provides supplemental fiber and aids digestion. Does NOT effectively control nuisance algae.

goodWith: mid-water and upper-level tetras (e.g. cardinal tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
small peaceful cichlids sharing similar water parameters (e.g. Apistogramma spp.)
other Loricariids occupying different territories (with adequate cave space)
avoidWith: other plecos in confined tanks without multiple dedicated cave territories
large aggressive cichlids that may outcompete at feeding time
fish requiring alkaline hard water (incompatible parameters)
very small nano fish that may be harassed
Green Phantom Pleco (L200) in small tanks — conspecific-style territorial conflict

summary: The Blue Phantom Pleco (L128) is a striking undescribed Loricariid from Venezuela’s fast-flowing Orinoco tributaries, distinguished by its dark body covered with iridescent blue-white spots. A carnivore-leaning omnivore that does NOT graze algae, it requires strong flow, rocky caves, and protein-rich foods. Captive-bred specimens are increasingly available. Intermediate care.
careNotes:
The Blue Phantom Pleco (L128) is an undescribed species of Hemiancistrus from the upper Rio Orinoco drainage in Venezuela, primarily associated with the Río Cuao and surrounding fast-water tributaries. It is closely related to the Green Phantom Pleco (L200) and other “Phantom” pleco species in the Hemiancistrus complex. Unlike the large common plecos (Pterygoplichthys spp.) sold in the trade, L128 is a carnivore-leaning omnivore — it does not effectively control algae and should not be purchased for that purpose. Its diet should center on protein-rich sinking foods: high-quality carnivore pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, and mysis shrimp, supplemented with occasional blanched vegetables and biofilm grazing from rocks and driftwood.

Replicating Orinoco rapids conditions is essential: water must be warm (26–30°C), soft (gH 2–12), slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.2), and highly oxygenated with strong directional flow from a powerhead or wavemaker. Water quality must be pristine — this species is sensitive to nitrate accumulation and dissolved organic buildup; weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are recommended.

Territorial aggression between conspecifics and similarly-shaped Loricariids is pronounced. A single specimen should be provided with at least two or three cave-style hiding spots (ceramic cave tubes, slate caves, or coconut shells) to allow a secure territory. Males are particularly intolerant of rival males; if keeping multiple specimens, a significantly larger tank (300+ L) with multiple non-overlapping territories is required.

Captive-bred specimens, increasingly available from specialist breeders, are hardier and more disease-resistant than wild-caught fish and are strongly preferred. Wild-caught individuals may require an extended acclimatisation period. The trade designation L128 is an “L-number” assigned by hobbyist publications before formal taxonomic description; the species remains scientifically undescribed as of 2026.
breedingNotes: Breeding is possible in well-maintained aquaria and has been achieved by specialist keepers, though it is not common. The Blue Phantom Pleco is a cave spawner: the male selects and guards a cave, and the female deposits adhesive eggs inside. The male fans and guards the clutch through hatching (typically 5–7 days at 28°C). Fry are relatively large at hatching and can accept finely crushed sinking pellets and baby brine shrimp within a few days. Conditioning the breeding pair with live and frozen protein-rich foods and performing regular cool-water changes to simulate the rainy season can trigger spawning. A dedicated breeding tank with soft, acidic water, strong flow, and multiple cave options improves success rates. Breedingdifficulty is considered moderate to difficult due to specific conditioning requirements.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hemiancistrus sp. (L128)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rio Orinoco drainage, Venezuela), water parameters, adult size (~20 cm), diet (carnivore-leaning omnivore, biofilm and meaty foods), tank requirements (strong flow, rocky caves, 200+ L), territorial behavior, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Hemiancistrus sp.
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), native range confirmation (Orinoco basin, Venezuela), and size data. L-number undescribed species; FishBase entry reflects genus-level data supplemented by L128-specific hobbyist records.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for trade-level care context: feeding guidance emphasizing protein-rich diet for carnivore-leaning plecos, cave requirements, water flow needs, and confirmation that L128 is not an algae grazer. General Loricariid husbandry principles applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — L-number Pleco Guide
additional.notes: Referenced for L128 trade designation context, relationship to L200 Green Phantom Pleco, Orinoco rapids biotope requirements, and increasing captive-bred availability from specialist breeders. Confirmed carnivore-leaning diet and territorial cave-dwelling behavior.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish — Hemiancistrus sp. (L128)
additional.notes: Specialist catfish database. Confirmed L128 designation, Venezuelan Orinoco origin (Río Cuao and nearby drainages), soft acidic warm water requirements, cave-spawning breeding behavior, and hobbyist reports of successful captive breeding. Cross-checked adult size (15–20 cm), water parameters, and carnivore-leaning diet.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner; male guards eggs and fry. Requires conditioning with live/frozen foods and cool-water changes to trigger spawning. Fry accept crushed pellets and baby brine shrimp. Captive breeding reported by specialists but not routine.
